title: Assignment problem (min cost matching) (Hungarian / min-cost flow).
description: Assignment problem (min cost matching) (Hungarian / min-cost flow).

examples.explanation: Permutations of [0,1]: [0,1]: cost=5+6=11; [1,0]: cost=8+3=11. Both give 11, first assignment [0,1] selected.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of assignments between n workers and n jobs, compute the total cost for each permutation, and select the minimum. This is straightforward but impractical for large n due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Assignment Problem
ladders.desc: Complete solution: generate all permutations of assignments between n workers and n jobs, compute total cost for each, and select minimum.

explanation.prose: The brute force approach solves the assignment problem by exhaustively checking every possible assignment between workers and jobs. Intuition: Since each worker must be assigned to exactly one job and each job to exactly one worker, assignments correspond to permutations of job indices assigned to workers in order. By generating all n! permutations, we guarantee checking the optimal assignment. Step-by-step: 1) Generate all permutations of [0,1,..,n-1] representing job assignments for workers 0 to n-1. 2) For each permutation, compute total cost by summing cost[worker][job] for each worker-job pair. 3) Track minimum cost and corresponding assignment. Time: O(n! * n) because n! permutations each requiring O(n) cost calculation. Space: O(n) for storing current permutation and best assignment.

connectsTo.reason: This uses permutation generation (L1) as the key subroutine

commonMistakes: Not handling n=0 or n=1 correctly

examples.trace: Permutations of [0,1]: [0,1]: cost=5+6=11; [1,0]: cost=8+3=11. Both give 11, first assignment [0,1] selected.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of a list of n elements.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine generates all possible orderings (permutations) of n distinct elements, which is essential for brute force assignment. Intuition: Each permutation represents a unique assignment of jobs to workers. By systematically exploring all n! orderings, we ensure no assignment is missed. Step-by-step using backtracking: 1) Start with an empty current permutation and list of unused elements. 2) Recursively: pick an unused element, add to current permutation, mark as used, recurse. 3) When current permutation has n elements, record it. 4) Backtrack by removing last element and marking it unused. Time: O(n! * n) to generate n! permutations each of length n. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and current permutation.

connectsTo.reason: This generates the assignments checked in L0

commonMistakes: Not handling duplicates correctly (not needed here as elements are distinct indices)
explanation.codePattern: Use recursion with a visited array or swap-based algorithm

examples.trace: Start: perm=[], unused={0,1,2}. Pick 0: perm=[0], unused={1,2}. Pick 1: perm=[0,1], unused={2}. Pick 2: perm=[0,1,2] -> record. Backtrack: perm=[0,1], unused={2}. Backtrack: perm=[0], unused={1,2}. Pick 2: perm=[0,2], unused={1}. Pick 1: perm=[0,2,1] -> record. Continue similarly for other branches.

Start: perm=[], unused={'A','B'}. Pick 'A': perm=['A'], unused={'B'}. Pick 'B': perm=['A','B'] -> record. Backtrack: perm=['A'], unused={'B'}. Backtrack: perm=[], unused={'A','B'}. Pick 'B': perm=['B'], unused={'A'}. Pick 'A': perm=['B','A'] -> record.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Total Cost for an Assignment
ladders.desc: Given an assignment permutation, compute total cost by summing worker-job costs.

explanation.prose: This core logic calculates the total cost for a specific assignment, which is the heart of evaluating each permutation in brute force. Intuition: The assignment is represented as a permutation where index i is worker i and value perm[i] is the job assigned to that worker. Total cost is the sum of individual costs from the cost matrix. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize total=0. 2) For each worker i from 0 to n-1: total += cost[i][perm[i]]. 3) Return total. Time: O(n) for iterating through n workers. Space: O(1) aside from input.

connectsTo.reason: This computation is done for each permutation in L0

examples.trace: Worker 0: job=0, cost=1. Worker 1: job=2, cost=1. Worker 2: job=1, cost=1. Sum: 1+1+1=3.
Worker 0: job=1, cost=8. Worker 1: job=0, cost=3. Sum: 8+3=11.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Over List Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through indices of a list to access elements.

explanation.prose: This building block involves iterating through the indices of a list, which is fundamental for accessing elements in sequences like cost matrices or permutations. Intuition: In programming, lists are indexed starting from 0, and looping through indices allows systematic access to each element. Step-by-step: For a list of length n, use a loop (e.g., for i in range(n)) to generate indices 0,1,..,n-1. Within the loop, access list[i] to get the element. Time: O(n) for n iterations. Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This iteration is used in L2 to sum costs for each worker

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access 2D List Element
ladders.desc: Access an element in a 2D list using row and column indices.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves accessing a specific element in a two-dimensional list (matrix), which is crucial for retrieving costs in the assignment problem. Intuition: A 2D list is a list of lists, where the first index selects the row (outer list) and the second index selects the column (element within that row). Step-by-step: Given cost matrix cost, row index i, and column index j, access cost[i][j]. Ensure indices are within bounds: 0 <= i < len(cost) and 0 <= j < len(cost[i]). Time: O(1). Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This operation is used in L2 to get cost for each worker-job pair

commonMistakes: or assuming rectangular matrix without validation

examples.trace: cost[0] is [1,2,3]. cost[0][2] is the third element: 3.
cost[1] is [30,40]. cost[1][0] is the first element: 30.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — List and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand lists as ordered collections and access elements by index.

explanation.prose: This foundation concept introduces lists, which are ordered collections of items, and indexing, which allows accessing specific elements by their position. Intuition: A list is like a sequence of boxes numbered starting from 0; indexing lets you look inside a specific box. Step-by-step: 1) Define a list, e.g., my_list = [10, 20, 30]. 2) Access elements: my_list[0] gives 10 (first element), my_list[1] gives 20, etc. 3) Indices must be integers and within range 0 to len(my_list)-1. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) to store list.

connectsTo.reason: This basic list knowledge is required for accessing elements in 2D lists

commonMistakes: or confusing lists with other data structures

examples.trace: List has elements at positions: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15. Index 1 accesses the second element: 10.
List has elements at positions: 0->'apple', 1->'banana', 2->'cherry'. Index 0 accesses the first element: 'apple'.

approaches.name: Hungarian Algorithm O(n³)
approaches.summary: Use the Hungarian algorithm, which reduces the cost matrix through row and column operations to find an optimal assignment. It efficiently solves the assignment problem by iteratively improving a feasible solution.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Hungarian Algorithm for Minimum Cost Assignment
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the Hungarian algorithm to solve the assignment problem with O(n³) time complexity.

explanation.prose: The Hungarian algorithm solves the assignment problem by transforming the cost matrix through row and column operations to find an optimal matching. The intuition is that subtracting constants from rows and columns doesn't change the optimal assignment, allowing us to create a matrix with zeros where assignments can be made. The algorithm works in phases: 1) Reduce the matrix by subtracting row minima and column minima. 2) Find a maximum matching of zeros using König's theorem. 3) If the matching covers all rows, we have an optimal solution. 4) Otherwise, adjust the matrix using the minimum uncovered value and repeat. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Subtract the smallest element in each row from all elements in that row. 2) Subtract the smallest element in each column from all elements in that column. 3) Cover all zeros with minimum number of lines (rows+columns). 4) If number of lines equals n, optimal assignment exists at zero positions. 5) Otherwise, find smallest uncovered value, subtract it from all uncovered elements, and add it to elements covered twice. 6) Repeat from step 3.

connectsTo.reason: by using the zero covering subroutine as its core component

timeComplexity.note: due to the iterative covering process
